coredata.dc:description: The rapid growth of electric vehicles (EVs) has placed significant emphasis on the performance and safety of lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries, which serve as the primary energy storage solution. Effective thermal management is crucial to ensure battery safety, longevity, and efficiency, particularly under high discharge rates where excessive heat generation can compromise performance. This study examines the thermal behaviour of a 4 × 3 Li-ion nickel-cobalt-manganese (NCM)-21,700 battery module with a 12S1P configuration. The focus of this study is to mitigate the challenges associated with heat generation at high discharge rates. Experiments are performed on a single NCM-21,700 cell to estimate the heat generation during charge and discharge. Based on this heat generation data, several experiments are performed on the 4 × 3 battery module under different thermal environments. These environments include natural convection, battery case, and phase change material (PCM) inside the battery case. The objectives of this study are to mitigate heat generation and ensure effective thermal management. The findings of these experiments demonstrate that despite being simple and economical, natural convection is insufficient as the temperature of the battery module rises significantly at high discharge rates. Although standard EV applications do not exceed the range of 0.3–0.6 C-rate, our study is focussed on extreme conditions of 1C and 2C rates. The maximum battery cell temperature in the battery module was found to be 48 °C and 53 °C for 1C and 2C discharge rates, respectively. The maximum temperature difference in the battery module was found to be 6 °Cand 13 °C for 1C and 2C discharge rates, respectively. The maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference of the battery module for PCM were reduced to 20 % and 45.45 % compared to conventional natural convection, respectively for a 1C discharge rate. PCM-based cooling provides better thermal uniformity across the battery module, helps to avoid thermal hotspots, and provides even performance across the battery module. The maximum temperature in any battery module is observed at the central cell due to the lesser space for heat dissipation. The results underscore the need for specialized cooling techniques for enhanced battery life, safety, and performance of high-energy Li-ion batteries in EV applications. This study also offers insights into efficient thermal management techniques for EV battery modules.

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation of thermal characteristics of a 12S1P Li-ion NMC-21700 battery module under different environments for EV applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pandit Deendayal Energy University, Gandhinagar, Gujarat, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pandit Deendayal Energy University, Gandhinagar, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid growth of electric vehicles (EVs) has placed significant emphasis on the performance and safety of lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries, which serve as the primary energy storage solution. Effective thermal management is crucial to ensure battery safety, longevity, and efficiency, particularly under high discharge rates where excessive heat generation can compromise performance. This study examines the thermal behaviour of a 4 × 3 Li-ion nickel-cobalt-manganese (NCM)-21,700 battery module with a 12S1P configuration. The focus of this study is to mitigate the challenges associated with heat generation at high discharge rates. Experiments are performed on a single NCM-21,700 cell to estimate the heat generation during charge and discharge. Based on this heat generation data, several experiments are performed on the 4 × 3 battery module under different thermal environments. These environments include natural convection, battery case, and phase change material (PCM) inside the battery case. The objectives of this study are to mitigate heat generation and ensure effective thermal management. The findings of these experiments demonstrate that despite being simple and economical, natural convection is insufficient as the temperature of the battery module rises significantly at high discharge rates. Although standard EV applications do not exceed the range of 0.3–0.6 C-rate, our study is focussed on extreme conditions of 1C and 2C rates. The maximum battery cell temperature in the battery module was found to be 48 °C and 53 °C for 1C and 2C discharge rates, respectively. The maximum temperature difference in the battery module was found to be 6 °Cand 13 °C for 1C and 2C discharge rates, respectively. The maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference of the battery module for PCM were reduced to 20 % and 45.45 % compared to conventional natural convection, respectively for a 1C discharge rate. PCM-based cooling provides better thermal uniformity across the battery module, helps to avoid thermal hotspots, and provides even performance across the battery module. The maximum temperature in any battery module is observed at the central cell due to the lesser space for heat dissipation. The results underscore the need for specialized cooling techniques for enhanced battery life, safety, and performance of high-energy Li-ion batteries in EV applications. This study also offers insights into efficient thermal management techniques for EV battery modules.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: EVs are emerging as a vital solution in the drive to reduce environmental pollution and alleviate the effects of climate change. To reduce their carbon footprint and to gradually transition towards a fossil fuel-independent world, automakers are placing more emphasis on EVs as an alternative to ICEs [1]. EVs are a cleaner mode of transportation as they have zero tailpipe emissions which ensures lesser environmental impact [2]. To lower air pollution and preserve natural resources, governments have come up with stringent emission norms. The transition to EVs is one of the key steps to meet these norms. The decline in battery prices is one of the major reasons for the increasing uptake of EVs, which makes EVs affordable for a wide range of customers [3]. Continuous improvement in energy density, driving range, and overall performance is achievable because of advances in battery technology [4]. Additionally, the charging feasibility has also boosted the adoption of EVs with the help of expanding charging infrastructure [5]. To reduce the upfront cost for consumers, many organizations and governments are also providing subsidies and financial incentives. The transition to EVs also supports the global sustainability goals to create a greener energy economy. This support, along with developments in technology and increasing public awareness, is accelerating the shift to EVs. Extensive investments are made by major automakers in EV technology research to enhance battery performance, minimize charging times, and expand the charging network. This research will provide solutions to any ongoing challenge and promote further industry expansion. Overall, growth in the EV market is a major step towards a green and sustainable transportation system, making it a vital technology for a cleaner future of mobility [6].

Adopting an appropriate battery is essential for EVs to advance and become efficient, with different battery technologies providing unique benefits. Li-ion batteries have emerged as the most promising battery type because of their high power density, which is necessary for quick acceleration and performance, and greater energy density, which allows for longer driving ranges. Li-ion batteries are best suited for automotive applications as they offer a long cycle life and a low self-discharge rate [7,8]. Among Li-ion batteries, lithium NMC batteries are distinctive owing to their excellent balance of energy density, thermal stability, and safety. In NMC cells, while nickel enhances energy density, manganese and cobalt boost longevity and stability. These aspects make NMC a perfect choice to satisfy the high energy requirements of an EV [9]. Moreover, the 21700 cells offer significant benefits over the 18650 cells like higher capacity and energy density. This leads to longer driving ranges and more efficiency. Better thermal management is also made possible due to its increased surface area, improving overall safety. It is also easy to integrate the 21700 cells in the battery packs and modular designs present in the current EV market [10]. Therefore, adopting NMC-21700 cells in EVs meets the evolving requirements of the automobile industry.

Battery thermal management is essential in EVs, as elevated temperatures have adverse effects on the batteries like longevity, safety, and performance [11]. The ideal operating temperature range for Li-ion batteries is 20–45 °C. Within this range, optimal performance features like long cycle life, dependable discharge, and effective capacity are observed. Temperatures beyond this range, especially higher temperatures cause deterioration of battery material. This leads to increased internal resistance, decreased capacity, and a higher chance of thermal runaway [12]. Generally, Li-ion batteries can withstand maximum temperatures up to about 60 °C. If this threshold is exceeded, the battery may sustain serious damage. Extended exposure to high temperatures can intensify problems including electrode material degradation and electrolyte breakdown, which further cuts down battery life. Moreover, excessive heat can result in safety risks like leaks and thermal instability. It is also essential to maintain a temperature differential of <5 °C across cells in a single pack [13]. Therefore, maintaining battery temperatures within the recommended range is crucial for ensuring reliable performance and extending the battery's operational life. Table 1-1 represents the effect of temperatures in Li-ion batteries.

Maintaining optimal performance and extending the life of Li-ion batteries, especially in EVs, requires efficient battery thermal management [26]. Among the various cooling systems, air cooling is the simplest and most economical method, using airflow to dissipate heat through fans and ventilation ducts. Although air cooling is simple to use and requires less maintenance, it has certain drawbacks. Particularly, it is less effective at handling high-power or high-temperature situations, which can result in uneven temperature distribution and possible hot spots that can shorten battery life [27]. In contrast, liquid cooling systems provide a more effective solution by absorbing and transferring heat through channels or pipes inside the battery pack using a liquid coolant, usually a water-antifreeze mixture. In comparison to air cooling, this technique offers superior thermal conductivity and more consistent temperature regulation, preventing overheating and prolonging battery life [28]. The numerical model of a 53 Ah lithium-ion battery (LIB) with a hybrid system consisting of PCM and cold plates on top and bottom reduces the maximum battery cell temperature to 46.3 °C [29]. A 1D model is developed to simulate the thermal behavior of Li-ion batteries. These studies used the concept of systematically measuring extreme aspects of battery cells [30].

The study examined the three current rates of 1C, 0.4C, and 0.8C, which had maximum temperatures of 36 °C, 33 °C, and 26 °C, respectively, for a single cell [31]. For a 2-cell battery, the cell temperature increased by 43.7, 18.6, 16.5, and 5.8 °C for a 4C discharge using the natural cooling, forced convection, mineral oil, and SF33 cooling techniques, respectively [32]. Results from experiments on a battery comprising 8 prismatic cells show that pure PCM, pure liquid cooling, and the hybrid PCM-liquid cooling system can reduce the module's maximum temperature from 43 °C to 35.6 °C, 34.5 °C, and 34.8 °C for 3C rapid charging, [33]. The impact of change in coolant flow rate is more prominent on average battery cell temperature in air-cooling while it gives a higher average battery cell temperature difference for 12-cell prismatic batteries having U-type cooling arrangement [34]. The numerical and experimental studies on a 7S4P battery module at 1C and 2C discharge rates indicated that the maximum cell temperature was observed to decrease from 62 °C to 42 °C for 2C and for 1C it was observed to decrease from 42 °C to 32 °C when the air flow rate increases from 0.5 m/s to 2 m/s. It was also observed that as the air velocity rises from 0.5 to 2.0 m/s, the pumping power is seen to increase from 0.015 to 0.513 W [35].

However, as coolant reservoirs, pumps, and radiators are required, liquid cooling systems are more expensive and complex. There is also a chance of leakage, which could harm the battery or present safety problems [36]. With PCM cooling, surplus heat is absorbed, stored, and dissipated using materials that undergo a phase transition at a specific temperature. This is an innovative and efficient way of battery thermal management. By preserving a consistent temperature range within the battery pack, PCM cooling systems provide substantial advantages over air and liquid cooling. This method of cooling improves thermal stability, lowers the chances of thermal runaway, and needs less maintenance [37]. Therefore, PCM cooling is increasingly regarded as a superior choice for battery thermal management EVs.

In this study, we have conducted comprehensive experiments on a 4 × 3 battery module with a 12S1P configuration to evaluate its thermal performance in different environments. Experiments are performed on a single NCM-21700 cell and heat generation is calculated. Based on this data; to mitigate the heat generation, tests are performed on the 4 × 3 battery module under different environments. The experimental setup includes testing in natural convection, within a battery case, and PCM cooling inside the battery case. This approach allows for a thorough assessment of the battery's thermal behaviour across different cooling environments. The C-rate is a measure of the rate at which a battery is charged or discharged relative to its nominal capacity. For instance, a 1C rate means the battery will be fully charged or discharged in one hour, while a 0.5C rate indicates a two-hour cycle. The C-rate is the main reason for heat generation in battery Cells. The higher the C-rate, the more heat will be generated by the battery cell. The novelty of our study is highlighted by the investigation of battery performance under extreme conditions, beyond the typical C-rate of 0.5–0.6C which is generally found in standard EV applications. By examining discharge rates of 1C and 2C, we aim to explore the battery's behaviour under more demanding scenarios that could occur during high-performance operations or rapid-discharging conditions. This detailed examination provides critical insights into the battery's thermal management requirements and performance limits, contributing valuable data for the development of more efficient and reliable thermal management systems for advanced EV applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat generation model for NCM-21700 cell
ce:section.ce:para:
The heat generated within a battery cell can be determined using its electro-thermal model, which accounts for both electrical and thermal behaviour during operation. The electro-thermal model for the NCM-21700 Li-ion cell consists of two key heat sources that arise during charging and discharging cycles. The heat generated in the cell includes both Joule heating and reversible heating which is described by equation-(1). Joule heating results from the resistance encountered by the current flowing through the cell during charge and discharge, with the heat generated being directly proportional to the current and the cell's internal resistance, as shown in equation-(2). Reversible heating refers to the heat generated from the exothermic and endothermic reactions occurring within the cell during charge and discharge, which is mentioned in the equation-(3).

Direct measurements of chemical characteristics like the number of electrons and entropy changes are frequently challenging and time-consuming. Consequently, combined values for these factors based on various SOC are provided by previous studies [38,39]. These equations form the basis of the electro-thermal model and offer a comprehensive overview of the NCM-21700 Li-ion battery cell's thermal behaviour under different charge and discharge scenarios [40,41]. Convection heat dissipation from the cell's surface to the surrounding air is provided in equation-(4) and the net accumulated heat is shown in equation-(5) [42].

The main objectives of this investigation are to study the real-world EV battery by creating a prototype and experimentally investigating its thermal characteristics in practical environments with or without battery thermal management. The focus is to study the maximum battery module temperature and the temperature non-uniformity among battery cells for various environments. The different environments explored in this study are as follows.
•
Natural Convection – It demonstrates the scenario when the EV is in stationary condition and the generated heat is dissipated directly to the atmosphere. It is the ideal situation when no cooling strategies are implemented to a standstill EV.
•
Battery Case – This scenario demonstrates the ideal scenario of a real-world EV battery where the battery is placed inside a plastic case without any cooling system. In this study, the battery case acts almost as an adiabatic wall. The generated heat is trapped inside the battery case which replicates the practical aspects of EV battery.
•
PCM cooling – PCM is used as a passive thermal management method to ensure the maximum temperature and the temperature difference among the cells are in optimum ranges. Battery cells are placed in the battery case which is surrounded by PCM. This study provides a comprehensive view of the employability of PCM as a cooling method in EV batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup of single NCM-21700 cell
ce:section.ce:para:
In this experimental setup, the charging and discharging cycle is performed on a single NMC-21700 cell under natural convection and inside an insulated enclosure as shown in Fig. 2-1 . The program for this cycling includes charging the cell with a 0.5C rate followed by 10 min of rest. Further, the cell is discharged with a 1C or 2C rate based on the experimental requirement. The cutoff voltage for charging is set at the maximum voltage of the cell, i.e., 4.2 V, while for discharge it is set at 2 .8V. The charging and discharging methods employed in this set-up are CC-CV and CCD respectively. The charging with CC-CV specifies that the Battery Management System (BMS) initially charges the battery module with the required constant current (CC). Once the pre-set voltage (4.2 V) is achieved, the BMS charges the battery cell with a constant voltage (CV). The current drops gradually during CV charging. The charging ends when the current falls below the preset current (1.5 mA). The CC-CV charging ensures the full capacity of battery cells is utilized. The discharging is carried out at the required constant current (CC). K-type thermocouples are used to monitor the temperatures on the cell surface as well as inside the enclosure throughout the cycling process. The thermocouples are connected to a datalogger, which includes a built-in cold junction sensor for ambient temperature measurements. The cell tester and datalogger are integrated with a computer for data control and recording, ensuring a precise collection of temperature data under different conditions. Specifications for the battery cell are represented in Table 2-1 . Based on experimental data, this study uses energy balance equations-(6) - (8) to calculate the instantaneous heat generation of a cell in an insulated box [43].

The experimental setup for the 4 × 3 geometry battery module is illustrated in Fig. 2-2 . A spacing factor of 1.1 is selected for this module, as it provides effective thermal management with PCM [44]. Fig. 2-3 represents the 3D model of the arrangement of cells in the 4 × 3 battery module. The module consists of 12 NCM-21700 cells arranged in a 12S1P electrical configuration, where 12 cells are connected in series and form a single parallel group. This battery module has a maximum voltage of 50.4 V, a nominal voltage of 43.2 V, and a nominal capacity of 5Ah. The battery module is integrated with a BMS to ensure uniform charge and discharge across all cells. The BMS provides the balancing of charge in cells, thereby promoting longevity and consistent performance of the battery pack. The outputs from the battery are connected to the cycler for programmed testing.

The software available with the battery cycler allows precise programming of the charge and discharge cycles as per the experimental requirement. In this study, the CC-CV method is used for charging and the CCD method is used for discharging. The program was set for a 0.5C (2.5A) charge, followed by a 10-minute rest. Further, it was programmed for a 1C (5A) or 2C (10A) discharge based on the experimental requirement. The cutoff voltage for a single cell is considered as 4.2 V and 2 .8V. Accordingly, the battery module is cycled within a voltage range of 33.6 V to 50 .4V. This program is consistently applied across all testing environments. The specifications of the battery cycler are presented in Table 2-2 .

For thermal monitoring, K-type thermocouples are strategically placed on the cell surfaces and within the PCM as required by the experiment. In the Natural Convection and Closed Chamber experiments, six temperature sensors are placed on the surfaces of selected cells to capture their thermal behaviour. In the experiment involving the battery module with PCM, an additional six sensors are placed within the PCM to monitor its temperature at various points. The positioning of these sensors is depicted in Fig. 2-4 . A separate sensor is used to record the ambient temperature. To monitor and record the temperature data, all sensors are connected to a datalogger, ensuring accurate and continuous thermal profiling of the battery module under different cooling conditions. The datalogger has 16 channels with a USB output logging facility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment with battery module under natural convection
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, the 4 × 3 battery module is placed in an open environment, away from any sources of forced airflow, to ensure natural convection conditions as shown in Fig. 2-5 . The charging and discharging processes are conducted under ambient temperature. The module is first cycled with the predefined program, which includes a 1C discharge rate. After the initial cycle, the battery is allowed to cool down naturally until it attains ambient temperature. The program is then adjusted to perform the cycling with a 2C discharge rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment with battery module in a battery case
ce:section.ce:para: The 4 × 3 battery module is placed within a battery case made of acrylic material with 5 mm thick walls to replicate an actual EV battery scenario with a plastic case. In this case, the majority of the heat generated during operation is trapped within it, limiting heat dissipation to the surroundings. Similar to the natural convection experiment, the cycler is initially programmed for a 1C discharge rate. After the initial cycle, the battery is allowed to rest until it returns to ambient conditions. The cycler is then reprogrammed to cycle the battery module with a 2C discharge rate. This setup is illustrated in Fig. 2-6 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment with battery module in PCM
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, a PCM is strategically selected to provide efficient cooling for the battery module. The specifications of the PCM are detailed in Table 2-3 . The PCM is melted externally and then carefully poured into the battery case containing the battery module. Acrylic material is selected for the case to allow visual observation of the PCM during its phase change process. The main objective is to control the maximum battery cell temperature below the critical temperature. A wide range of PCMs were considered for the BTMS application. Only paraffin-based organic PCM was considered for the BTMS as inorganic PCM and acid-based organic PCM will corrode the battery cell as it is exposed to the battery cell surface which puts inorganic PCM and acid-based organic PCM in the absolute case for the BTMS. The inorganic PCMs and acid-based organic PCMs were ignored for their corrosive nature and long-term stability although they offer a large latent heat. To operate the battery cell at a safe temperature limit of 55–60 °C, the PCM having a melting range of 34–46 is selected. Such PCM will give the full advantage of limiting the temperature for any operating conditions. Based on the latent heat, specific heat, and ease of availability the paraffin-based PCM OM36 is selected for the BTMS.

Since the PCM is utilized to control the operating temperature of the battery cells by absorbing the heat from its surface, the PCM-based BTMS can also be implemented for the larger battery module comprising of any form factor i.e. 18,650 or 21700.

To monitor the thermal behaviour within the PCM, six additional temperature sensors are placed inside the PCM, as discussed in the above Section 2.2. Similar to the previous experiments, the battery module is first cycled with a 1C discharge rate. After allowing the battery to return to ambient temperature, the module is then cycled again with a 2C discharge rate. The setup for this experiment is depicted in Fig. 2-7 .
Systematic errors associated with the parameters are presented in Table 2-4 , with the accuracy specifications derived from the manufacturers' datasheets.

Repeatability refers to the variations in results that occur despite maintaining consistent experimental and thermal conditions. To account for these fluctuations, each experiment is conducted multiple times (typically four to five). After these repetitions, Moffat's method [45] is used to analyze the uncertainties and evaluate the reliability of the experimental results. The average uncertainty across all measurements from the instruments is calculated using Eq. (9).

The repeatability of the primary parameters is provided in Table 2-5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat generation in a single NMC-21700 cell
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3-1 depicts the instantaneous heat generation in an NCM-21700 cell during a 1C discharge rate. Initially, at the start of the discharge, heat generation rapidly increases to 0.82 W at 250 s. This initial surge is due to the high current draw causing a significant rise in internal resistance and electrochemical activity, which results in elevated thermal output. Following this peak, the heat generation gradually decreases to approximately 0.43 W at 1000 s. This represents the stable internal conditions and a decrease in exothermic reaction rate as the cell accustoms to the discharge process. Due to a continuous electrochemical reaction, a minor rise to 0.45 W is observed at 1300 s. Reduction in internal resistance causes a drop to 0.30 W at 1700 s. Further, the rise is seen to 0.55 W at 2250s and 06.2 W at 2700 s which is attributed to the increase in internal resistance. A drop in heat generation is observed after this and it reaches 0.47 W at 3000 s. This is due to the lower capacity left in the cell at the latter part of the discharge. As the cell approaches depletion, the heat generation rises to 1.02 W because of the continuous current draw even at lower capacity. The discharge cycle completes at 3300 s. The average heat generation of the cell during this cycle is noted to be 0.53 W.

Fig. 3-2 depicts the instantaneous heat generation in an NCM-21700 cell during a 2C discharge rate. The heat generation initially rises sharply to 3.45 W at 100 s due to the high current draw increasing internal resistance and electrochemical activity. It then decreases to 2.95 W at 400 s as the cell begins to stabilize, followed by a steeper drop to 2.3 W at 500 s as internal processes adjust to the high discharge rate. A slight increase to 2.35 W at 650 s reflects ongoing electrochemical activity. Heat generation then drops to 1.7 W at 1000 s due to reduced internal resistance. It rises to 2.0 W in the 1200 s, then fluctuates between 1.8 W and 1.9 W from 1300 to 1400 s as internal conditions vary. Finally, it decreases to 1.5 W at the 1500 s before rising to 1.9 W by the end of the cycle, driven by heightened resistance and intensified reactions as the cell depletes. The discharge cycle was completed in the 1600 s. From this data, the average heat generation for the 2C discharge is noted at 2 .25W.

The results yielded in the experiments are presented and discussed in this section. Fig. 3-3 illustrates the relationship between Voltage and DOD during a complete charge-discharge cycle. Initially, when the battery module is idle, the voltage stabilizes to around 35.3 V. As the charging process begins, a rapid rise in voltage is observed from 0 to 5 % of charge (100–95 % DOD), which can be attributed to the initial rapid replenishment of charge in the cell's active material. Following this, the voltage increases gradually, reaching the nominal voltage of 43.2 V at approximately 25–30 % SOC (75–70 % DOD). The voltage continues to rise steadily until the upper cutoff voltage of 50.4 V is reached, indicating that the battery module is fully charged. During the subsequent 10 min rest period, the voltage slightly drops to around 48V. The discharge process then commences, with the nominal voltage being achieved at approximately 45–50 % of the discharge process. Further, the battery module continues to discharge until it reaches the lower cutoff voltage of 33.6 V. Similar voltage curves are observed for both the 1C and 2C discharge cycles.

Fig. 3-4 represents the relationship between DOD and the average battery cell temperature during a cycle with a 1C discharge across the environments of natural convection, a battery case, and a PCM-based system. At the start of the cycle, the ambient temperature in all three cases is within the range of 32–34 °C. Under natural convection, as the charging process begins, the temperature initially rises from 34 °C to 39 °C between 0 and 20 % of the charge cycle. This initial rise is due to the heat generated by internal resistance and electrochemical reactions as the cells absorb charge. As the charge progresses from 20 to 60 %, the temperature increases gradually, reaching 41–42 °C. This gradual rise is attributed to the dissipated heat being more effectively balanced by ambient cooling. For the remainder of the charge cycle, the temperature stabilizes at around 42 °C. During the 10-minute rest period, the temperature decreases by approximately 1–1.5 °C as the heat dissipates into the surrounding environment. When the discharge process starts, the temperature continuously rises from 41 °C at the beginning to 52 °C by the end of the cycle, driven by the increased heat generation due to higher discharge rates.

In the battery case scenario, the temperature rise is more pronounced compared to natural convection due to the limited heat dissipation, as heat is trapped within the chamber. During the charging cycle, the temperature continuously rises, reaching 47 °C by the end of the charge phase. During the rest period, there is a minor decrease to 45 °C, indicating some heat dissipation but still within the confined environment. As the discharge process begins, the temperature steadily increases, peaking at 59 °C by the end of the cycle, a result of the combined effects of high discharge current and inadequate heat escape, which leads to higher thermal accumulation.

When using PCM, the temperature profile exhibits a distinct behaviour. The temperature rises to 34 °C during the initial 0.15 % of the charge cycle. The temperature remains constant after this stage until 40 % of the charge cycle. This constant temperature is due to the PCM's capability to absorb latent heat during its phase change process at 34–35 °C. The thermal buffer provided by PCM during this phase change process delays the further temperature rise. For the remaining part of the cycle, the temperature gradually rises to 37 °C. During the rest period, there is a minor temperature drop, and during the discharge cycle, the temperature gradually increases, reaching 42–43 °C by the end of the cycle. The graph demonstrates that the use of an appropriate PCM can effectively control the temperature of the battery within permissible limits. Moreover, the PCM helps in moderating the temperature rise, making it more gradual compared to other scenarios where the temperature increase during discharge is much more rapid. The melting of PCM after 1C discharge is depicted in Fig. 3-5 .

Fig. 3-6 illustrates the relationship between DOD and the average battery cell temperature during a cycle with a 2C discharge across the environments of natural convection, a battery case, and a PCM-based system. In the natural convection scenario, the battery temperature initially rises from 37 °C to 41 °C during the first 20 % of the charge cycle. From 40 % to 70 % of the charge cycle, the temperature gradually increases to 45 °C. A slight temperature drop is observed between 70 % and 100 % of the charge cycle, due to the reduced current demand in the later stages of charging, which decreases the rate of heat generation. During the rest period, the temperature further decreases to 43 °C as the battery stabilizes and dissipates some of the accumulated heat. During the discharge cycle, a continuous and rapid rise in temperature is observed, with the temperature peaking at 71 °C. This rapid temperature increase is due to the higher heat generation during the 2C discharge rate, which exceeds the natural cooling capacity of the surrounding air.

In the battery case setup, the battery temperature rises more significantly, starting from 36 °C and reaching 47 °C throughout the first 80 % of the charge cycle. The temperature remains almost constant for the next 10 % of the charge cycle before increasing by approximately 0.5 °C towards the end of the cycle. A decline of 1–1.5 °C is observed during the rest period, due to the heat dissipation to the surrounding chamber walls. During the discharge cycle, the temperature rises steadily, reaching 78 °C by the end of the cycle. The confined environment of the battery case intensifies the heat accumulation, leading to a higher temperature rise compared to natural convection.

In contrast, the PCM-based system demonstrates a more controlled temperature profile. The temperature gradually rises from 29 °C to 34 °C throughout the charge cycle, with negligible variation during the rest period, as the battery module is at 34 °C, initiating the PCM's phase change. During the discharge process, the temperature increases to 39 °C at 50 % DOD and continues to rise, reaching 47 °C by the end of the cycle. The PCM effectively absorbs the heat generated during the charge and discharge cycles, maintaining the battery temperature within a safer and manageable range. This controlled temperature rise prevents thermal runaway and also enhances the overall efficiency and longevity of the battery module compared to natural convection and battery case scenarios, where the temperature increases are more pronounced and less controlled. The melting of PCM after 2C discharge is depicted in Fig. 3-7 .

The results presented in Fig. 3-8 provide key insights into the temperature distribution across the battery cells during the cycles with a 1C discharge. In the case of natural convection, the temperature difference initially rises to 4 °C by 35–40 % of the charge cycle. This increase is attributed to the uneven heat dissipation across the cells, where the centrally located cells tend to retain more heat compared to those on the periphery. Following this initial rise, the temperature difference fluctuates and drops to 3 °C at 45 % of the charge cycle, likely due to brief periods of thermal equilibrium within the module. As the charging continues, the temperature difference increases again, fluctuating between 4 and 5 °C until the end of the charge cycle. During the discharge process, the temperature difference rises to 5 °C by 35–40 % of the discharge cycle, as heat generation intensifies with higher discharge rates. Subsequently, it fluctuates between 5 and 7 °C for 40–60 % of the discharge cycle and eventually increases to 11 °C by the end of the cycle, with minor fluctuations along the way. This rise during discharge is indicative of the increased thermal stress as the cells deplete their charge.

In the battery case scenario, the initial temperature profile mirrors that of natural convection, with similar trends observed up to 60 % of the charge cycle. However, after this point, the temperature difference increases more rapidly, reaching 6–7 °C by the end of the charge cycle. This accelerated increase is due to the restricted airflow and heat accumulation within the battery case, which prevents effective heat dissipation. During the rest period, a 1–1.5 °C drop in temperature difference is observed, likely because of the brief pause in active thermal generation. However, during the discharge process, the temperature difference fluctuates and increases, peaking at 11.5 °C by the end of the discharge cycle. The battery case increases temperature disparities due to the confined environment, leading to a more uneven thermal distribution across the cells.

In contrast, the PCM scenario demonstrates superior thermal management. Throughout the charge cycle, the temperature difference does not exceed 2 °C, as the PCM effectively absorbs and redistributes the heat generated, maintaining uniform temperature across the cells. Even during the discharge cycle, the maximum temperature difference is limited to 6 °C, with fewer fluctuations compared to the other two scenarios. The PCM's ability to absorb latent heat during its phase change significantly contributes to this uniformity, preventing rapid temperature increases and ensuring a more stable thermal environment. This highlights the benefit of PCM in not only reducing the overall temperature rise but also in maintaining uniformity in the battery cells' temperature distribution, which is crucial for enhancing the longevity and performance of the battery module.

Fig. 3-9 presents the relationship between DOD and the temperature difference among battery cells during a 2C discharge cycle in natural convection, a battery case, and PCM-based cooling. In the natural convection scenario, the temperature difference remains between 0 °C and 3 °C until 40 % of the charge cycle. This stability can be attributed to the relatively low heat generation during the early charging phase, which allows natural convection to effectively dissipate the heat. As the charge progresses, the temperature difference increases to between 3 °C and 5.5 °C up to 80 % DOD, due to rising internal resistance and increased heat generation as the cells reach higher states of charge. Toward the end of the cycle, the temperature difference slightly drops and fluctuates between 4.5 °C and 5 °C, likely because of the tapering off of charging current and the stabilization of heat generation. During the discharge phase, the temperature difference escalates with fluctuations, peaking at around 22 °C by the end of the cycle, due to the high discharge rate leading to significant heat buildup that cannot be mitigated only by natural convection.

In the battery scenario, the overall trend mirrors that of natural convection, with the key difference being a marginally higher temperature difference throughout the cycle. This increase is due to the restricted heat dissipation within the closed environment, which limits the effectiveness of natural convection. Despite these conditions, the maximum temperature difference at the end of the discharge phase is slightly lower at 20.5 °C compared to the natural convection case, because of the minor difference in ambient temperatures during both experiments.

In the PCM cooling scenario, the temperature fluctuations are significantly dampened compared to the other two cases. During the charging phase, the temperature difference remains tightly controlled between 0 °C and 2 °C. During the discharging phase, the temperature difference increases but remains relatively much lower, reaching only about 13 °C at the end of the cycle. At 50 % DOD, the difference is around 7 °C, showcasing the PCM's effectiveness in reducing temperature gradients and enhancing thermal uniformity within the battery pack. Although this temperature difference is still significant, it is important to consider that this analysis is based on a 2C discharge condition, which is not representative of a typical EV operation. Under realistic discharge rates of 0.6C or lower, PCM cooling would manage temperature uniformity even more effectively, providing substantial benefits in maintaining the battery's thermal stability and prolonging its operational life.

Fig. 3-10 illustrates the relationship between the DOD and the maximum battery cell temperature within the central region of the battery module. This central cell, due to its position, experiences the highest temperature since the heat generated has limited avenues for dissipation. The data reveals that, in both 1C and 2C discharge scenarios, the incorporation of PCM significantly mitigates the temperature rise. For the 1C discharge cycle, the maximum temperature in a battery case peaks at 65 °C, while the integration of PCM reduces it to 46 °C. Similarly, during the 2C discharge cycle, the maximum temperature in the battery case reaches 90 °C but is effectively lowered to 53 °C with PCM implementation. This temperature reduction can be attributed to the PCM's latent heat absorption capability, which effectively absorbs and dissipates excess heat generated during high discharge rates. The PCM thus enhances thermal management by stabilizing the cell temperatures and preventing them from reaching detrimental levels.

Fig. 3-11 illustrates the relationship between the average temperature of the battery cells and the PCM with respect to DOD. During both 1C and 2C discharge cycles, the average temperature profile of the PCM closely follows that of the battery cells during the charging phase. This is attributed to the initial temperature range of 29–32 °C, which is below the PCM's melting point. Consequently, both the battery cells and PCM temperatures increase concurrently. Although the thermal conductivity of the PCM is lower, the geometrical design of the module facilitates effective thermal propagation.

As temperatures approach the PCM's melting range of 34–35 °C, both the battery cells and PCM temperatures rise gradually. This period is characterized by the PCM undergoing a phase transition, storing heat as latent heat. During the later stages, particularly in the discharging phase, the temperature rises rapidly. This happens as the PCM surrounding the cells directly has completed its phase transition, reduced its latent heat storage capacity and increased sensible heating. Thus, a noticeable temperature difference between the battery cells and PCM emerges.

For the 1C discharge cycle, by the end of the cycle, when the battery cell temperature is 43 °C, the average PCM temperature is 41 °C. In the 2C discharge cycle, with the battery cell temperature reaching approximately 46 °C, the PCM temperature averages around 44.5 °C. A higher magnitude of the average temperature difference between the battery cells and PCM is observed during the 2C discharge cycle as compared to the 1C cycle. This happens due to the higher heat generation rate of the cells during the 2C discharge which exceeds the capacity of the PCM to absorb and dissipate heat.

ce:section-title.ce:bold: Comparative study of various experiments conducted on 4 × 3 module

ce:section.ce:para: This section represents a comparison of the temperatures observed in the various environments within which the 4 × 3 battery module is tested under 1C and 2C discharge rate which is tabulated in Table 3-1 . It illustrates the temperature data for natural convection, the battery case, and the PCM environments for both 1C and 2C discharge rates.

The average battery cell temperature for the Battery Module is computed by averaging the instantaneous temperature of all battery cells for the complete charging and discharging cycle. It shows the pivot temperature around which the thermal system works for a complete cycle and helps to estimate the temperature range of thermal management. The average maximum cell temperature for the Battery Module is computed by averaging the temperature of all the battery cells whose instantaneous temperature is maximum for the complete charging and discharging cycle. It shows the maximum temperature around which the thermal system operates and helps to estimate the upper-temperature range of thermal management. The average temperature difference for the Battery Module is computed by averaging the instantaneous temperature difference of battery cells for the complete charging and discharging cycle.

At a 1C discharge rate, the maximum cell temperature reaches its highest value of 65.5 °C in the battery case, indicating the least efficient cooling among the tested environments. This temperature drops to 60 °C through natural convection, as the ambient air movement provides moderate cooling. The application of PCM results in a major decrease in maximum temperature, with the cell temperature dropping to 48 °C. This indicates the better cooling capability of the PCM in these circumstances. PCM is effective in preserving uniformity in cell temperatures, as evidenced by the fact that the temperature differential across the cells is 11.5 °C in the battery case, 11 °C under natural convection, and substantially lower at 6 °C when PCM is employed.

The maximum cell temperature in the battery case reaches a critical 90 °C at a 2C discharge rate, indicating the maximum thermal stress on the battery. Natural convection helps to reduce the maximum temperature to 84 °C, though this still represents significant heating. The PCM provides the most effective thermal management, bringing the temperature down to 53 °C, which is a substantial reduction compared to the other environments. Regarding the temperature difference across the cells, the battery case scenario shows a variation of 20.5 °C, natural convection results in a slightly larger difference of 22 °C, and PCM cooling effectively minimizes the difference to 13 °C, illustrating its advantage in both lowering and equalizing cell temperatures under high-stress conditions.

The battery module for EV operates at 0.3C to 0.6C charge or discharge rate. The experiment of the battery module is carried out at 1C and 2C discharge rates which gives extreme operating conditions like rapid discharging. The findings show that the PCM limits the battery cell temperature to 48 °C and 53 °C for 1C and 2C discharge rates, respectively. The maximum battery cell temperature is controlled to a temperature limit of 55–60 °C with the use of PCM. The maximum operating temperature of PCM is 90 °C which is far away from the established maximum cell temperature limit of 55–60 °C. There will be very little chance for the proposed BTMS to reach the maximum operating temperature of PCM which is 90 °C or go beyond the established maximum cell temperature limit of 55–60 °C. The BTMS will operate below or in the range of the temperature limit of 55–60 °C. Therefore, there will be no case for such battery cell runaway, fire, or hazard for the proposed BTMS.

The PCM has met all the objectives i.e. maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference, for 1C which is the most extreme scenario in a real-world EV application. It reduces the average battery cell temperature from 42 °C to 34 °C. The average cell temperature difference is also reduced from 4.5 °C to 1.9 °C. Since the PCM provides effective thermal management using passive cooling for the current case, the same can be achieved in any battery pack with the 21700/18650 Li-ion NMC cylindrical battery cells.
The cost analysis of the battery module used in this study is presented in this section, highlighting the cost-effectiveness of the PCM-based thermal management system employed.

The PCM used in this study, OM35, is inexpensive and widely available. To optimize the quantity of PCM required, the spacing factor between cells is carefully determined based on our previous work [44], leading to an optimized spacing factor of 1.1. As a result, only 290 g of PCM is utilized in the battery module. Additionally, the cost of PCM accounts for approximately 4.77 % of the total battery module cost, demonstrating the affordability of this passive cooling approach.

Furthermore, the cost-effectiveness of the PCM-based thermal management system is highlighted by comparing it with the air-cooling method for BTMS discussed in ref [46]. It is reported that achieving a 2 m/s air velocity for cooling requires approximately 0.513 W of power. [32] For a battery with a voltage of 3.6 V and a capacity of 3.5 A, the total power is 12.6 W, meaning that 4.1 % of the battery's power is consumed solely for cooling. This significantly increases the operational cost of the air-cooling-based BTMS system.

In contrast, the PCM-based passive cooling system used in this study achieves the same level of cooling as air-cooled BTMS [35], but with zero power consumption for cooling. This not only reduces the energy overhead but also lowers the overall cost of the battery thermal management system, making it a more sustainable and economical solution for EV applications.

This study explored the heat generation of a single NCM-21700 cell and based on this data, thermal performance, and thermal management strategies of a 12-cell NCM-21700 battery module under different environments focusing on natural convection, battery case environment, and PCM systems are studied. The following is a summary of the findings.
•
There is a noticeable rise in heat generation with higher discharge rates, evident by the average heat generation of 2.25 W for a 2C discharge rate as compared to 0.53 W for a 1C discharge rate.
•
PCM reduces the temperature from 65.5 °C to 48 °C for 1C discharge and 90 °C to 53 °C for 2C discharge compared to battery case setup. PCM reduces the cell temperature difference from 11.5 °C to 6 °C for 1C discharge and 20.5 °C to 13 °C for 2C discharge compared to battery case setup. Thus, the latent heat capacity of PCM ensures stable battery temperature and longer battery life.
•
The use of PCM resulted in a 20 % reduction in maximum cell temperature at 1C compared to natural convection and a 26.7 % reduction compared to the battery case setup. At 2C, PCM cooling provided an even greater reduction of 36.9 % compared to natural convection and 41.1 % compared to the battery case setup.

In summary, this study highlights the importance of effective battery thermal management in enhancing both battery efficiency and safety. It also underscores the benefit of PCM, especially in applications with high discharge rates.

The proposed PCM-based BTMS gives effective temperature control for the battery module of 12S1P. Similar research can also be extended to the larger battery module like 15S11P. The thermal performance of battery cells can also be explored for the larger battery module.
